vol_name: Children use syntax to learn verb meanings
vol_description: Verb learning is clearly a function of observation of real-world contingencies; however, it is argued that such observational information is insufficient to account fully for vocabulary acquisition. This paper provides an experimental validation of Landau & Gleitman's (1985) syntactic bootstrapping procedure; namely, that children may use syntactic information to learn new verbs. Pairs of actions were presented simultaneously with a nonsense verb in one of two syntactic structures. The actions were subsequently separated, and the children (MA = 2;1) were asked to select which action was the referent for the verb. The children's choice of referent was found to be a function of the syntactic structure in which the verb had appeared.

Children with ASD recruited to be within 6 months of diagnosis, producing fewer than 50 words and no phrase speech (MLUs under 2.0)
TD children recruited to match this level of language production, so ended up being 12 months younger.

Visited 6 times in first 2 years of study, every 4 months.
At each visit:  30 minute play session with caregiver, 15 minutes of STAT (Stone et al., 2000) and 15 minutes free play.  These are combined in the dataset and transcripts.
Standardized tests included Mullen VR, EL, RL. FM at V1 and V5
ADOS at V1 and V6
Mullen VR at V4
CDI-I at V1 for all
CDI-II at V2 and V3 for all
CDI-III at V4 for all TD
Children with ASD shifted to CDI-III for the visit after the visit at which their CDI-II was over 500 words

Children viewed 3-4 IPL videos at each visit.  They were seated on their caregiver’s lap, on an experimenter’s lap, or by themselves, watching the videos projected on a large portable screen (Naigles & Tovar, 2013).  Adults wore headphones that played Beethoven symphonies while children were watching.
Visits 1 and 2: Word Order, Shape Bias, Noun Bias
	Cohort 2 saw a word order visit with 2 human children, cohort 3 saw a word order video with the horse and bird (Candan et al., 2012)
Visit 3: Shape Bias, Syntactic Bootstrapping (short), wh-questions
	For all visits, cohort 2 saw Seidl et al. (2000) wh-question video, cohort 3 saw Jyotishi et al. (2017) wh-question horse and bird video
Visit 4: Shape Bias, Syntactic Bootstrapping (long), wh-questions
Visit 5, ASD group: Shape Bias, Aspect short, Syntactic Bootstrapping long, wh-questions
Visit 5, TD group: Aspect short, Syntactic Bootstrapping long, wh-questions
Visit 6, ASD group: Shape Bias, Aspect long, wh-questions
Visit 6, TD group: Aspect long, Syntactic Bootstrapping long, wh-questions

Speech during the play sessions at all 6 visits was transcribed in CHAT and then ‘posted’ using CLAN.  
For visits 1-3, Joint attention was coded during the play sessions, for RJA, IJA, and PA (Naigles et al. 2016; Abdel-Aziz et al., 2018)
